chapters.title: Your immune system is your personal health army, protecting you at all times
chapters.text:
When you’re born, you have little to no immunity against the world and its germ-related threats. That’s why we don’t go around subjecting newborn babies to the outside world the moment they come out of the womb! However, the human immune system is a complex and potent mechanism that works to protect you against health-related threats that come your way within a short period of time.

Understanding the immune system has taken centuries. Even now, we still don’t know everything. The most basic explanation of the immune system dates as far back as Hippocrates. He came up with the idea that there are four different aspects to human immunity, and when one overruns the others, sickness occurs. Those elements were phlegm, blood, black bile, and yellow bile. Of course, we now know that immunity is far more sophisticated than that.

The immune system is silent. We can feel our lungs rising and falling and our hearts beating, but we can’t see or hear the vital work being done by the immune system.

The immune system is present all over your body and is a network of millions of cells and organs working together, including:
• Leukocytes (white blood cells)
• Cytokines (molecules)
• Lymph nodes
• Spleen
• Bone marrow

When your body notices a threat, it kickstarts an immune response, mainly in the form of inflammation. However, we also know that the immune system can work against itself, causing inflammation when it’s not really necessary. When there is too much inflammation within the body, you can become quite sick.

So, it’s vital to understand what you can do to boost your immunity towards viruses and illness and keep your immune system ticking along in the way it is supposed to.

Throughout this summary, you will learn many tricks to boost your natural immunity. The good news is that you can change your health for the better simply by focusing on positive habits and kicking out negative ones.

chapters.title: Washing hands properly remains the single best way to protect yourself against germs
chapters.text:
When you start to learn about how the immune system works, you’ll quickly notice that it’s incredibly sophisticated and complex. However, some of the ways to protect it are very simple indeed.

Since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, we’ve been told almost on a daily basis about the importance of washing hands, but it’s been important for as long as we’ve been living! The simple act of properly washing your hands can drastically reduce your chances of catching common illnesses, such as colds, flu cases, and in the current climate, even coronavirus.

Germs can be present on the hands without any grime or dirt visible to the naked eye. Handwashing is the only way to make sure that you’re not harboring any unwanted visitors!

The link between disease prevention and handwashing wasn’t discovered until the 19th century. At that time, many women fell ill very soon after giving birth. A Hungarian doctor named Dr. Semmelweis put two and two together and realized that there was a very firm link between postpartum fever in women and doctors who hadn’t washed their hands after performing autopsies or other procedures related to dead patients. These doctors were transferring germs to the women and causing the illness.

Hand-washing remains a cornerstone of modern-day infection control. ~ Dr. Jenna Macciochi

Handwashing is so important because many people are actually infectious before they start to show any signs of illness. You could have been infected with a cold and be able to pass it on to someone else before you begin to notice the tell-tale stuffy nose and sore throat. When you wash your hands effectively, you’re drastically reducing the chances of infecting other people.

Did you know? To wash your hands correctly, you should lather with soap and scrub for at least 20 seconds.

chapters.title: The immune system is complex and present in all areas of your body
chapters.text:
Unlike other systems in the human body, the immune system doesn’t stay in one place; it is present all over the body and interacts with all other systems regularly. The environment where you live and work can also affect how healthy or otherwise your immune system is, although it is very talented at adapting to different situations.

Inside your body, you have something called a microbiota, a collection of around 38 trillion microbes, our good bacteria cells that keep us healthy and well. These microbes help to balance your natural immunity.

You need a healthy balance of good bacteria to ward off the bad. When you don’t look after your microbiota, you allow the bad bacteria to outnumber the good.

The immune system is trained over time and has evolved to handle different illnesses and situations throughout the centuries. Many of the microbes within your gut have been there since you were born, and they work together with every other part of your body to keep you healthy. However, to develop stronger immunity throughout your life, you have to be exposed to specific outside threats to teach your immune system how to deal with them.

Dr. Jenna Macciochi tells us that almost everything we touch throughout any given day is covered in microbes. You can’t see them, and for the most part, they’re harmless. But there could be just one that could cause you an illness if your immune system isn’t firing on all cylinders.

Your microbiota needs to be looked after and cared for. It’s not a tiny feature; your microbes make up around 2kg of your body weight! So, it should be treated as your private health army.

One essential element of keeping your microbiota healthy is your diet. Most of us don’t eat enough fiber, but it’s vital for the health of the gut and, therefore, the microbiota. In addition, you also need plenty of zinc, vitamins A, C, D, and selenium. You can gain all this by focusing on a healthy, organic diet, free from too much fat and sugar.

It’s always better to get your essential vitamins and minerals from your diet and not from supplements.

chapters.title: Antibiotics may be lifesavers, but they also have downsides
chapters.text:
If you become sick with an infection and go to see your doctor, you may be prescribed a course of antibiotics to help fight the infection and get better. However, while antibiotics are certainly a huge blessing — having saved millions of lives since they were invented — they also come with a pretty significant downside. They literally destroy your microbiota.

It’s for this reason that you should only take antibiotics as prescribed and only when necessary.

Taking antibiotics too frequently can reduce their effectiveness and have a very negative effect on your natural immunity.

Of course, antibiotics are antimicrobial drugs that help stop an infection from multiplying or killing it at the source. But, by taking just one antibiotic, the good bacteria in your gut takes a real hit. It can also destroy some of the good flora and leave you open to other complications as a result.

It’s not just antibiotics that can affect your microbiota, as regular use of other drugs such as non-steroidal anti-inflammatories (NSAIDs), proton-pump inhibitors, and even Metformin can cause problems with your healthy bacteria. Despite that, you should always take your medication as prescribed by your doctor and look after your gut health with a healthy diet.

We also hear a lot about probiotics when it comes to protecting the gut. However, these aren’t that well researched and may not work the same way for everyone. Dr. Jenna Macciochi suggests it’s better to look after your gut naturally rather than by taking probiotic supplements. You can do this by:
• Eating fresh, organic produce.
• Having a good intake of fermented food, such as kefir, kimchi, or sauerkraut.
• Focusing on eating plant-based foods.
• Learning to read food labels to avoid foods that contain too much salt, fat, or sugar.

chapters.title: If you want to be healthy, you need to make sleep a priority
chapters.text:
We all live busy lives, and sometimes we assume that taking the time to rest makes us lazy. But that’s not the case. For your body to recover and repair itself, it needs sleep and rest regularly.

The shorter you sleep, the shorter your life. ~ Matthew Walker

The amount of sleep you get can drastically affect how you feel in yourself, but it also affects your immune system function. When your body doesn’t have the time it needs to repair itself, it’s always running on empty. There’s also a firm link between sleep and immunity. A prolonged lack of sleep can have very adverse effects on your immunity. A study completed in the 19th century showed that dogs subjected to total sleep deprivation died after a few days due to a complete breakdown of their immune system.

Just one night of not sleeping so well can reduce the number of killer cells that help to protect against viruses you encounter in your natural environment.

The average amount of sleep required for an adult is between seven to nine hours, but most people don’t get that every single night. The quality of sleep also matters. If you’re waking up constantly, you’re not resting, and your body isn’t able to go through the four stages of sleep required for proper rest and healing.

There are four stages of sleep, but the fourth stage is the dreaming stage, also known as REM (Rapid Eye Movement). This happens around 90 minutes after you fall asleep.

You can improve the amount of quality of sleep you’re getting by setting some healthy sleeping habits.
• Avoid using technological devices at night: the blue light emitted can upset your natural body clock, also called the circadian rhythm.
• Try to have a nap in the afternoon, if you can, to work off any sleep debt you might have accumulated.
• Make sure that your bedroom is between 60-65 F.
• Exercise during the day but finish at least two hours before you intend to sleep.
• Avoid anything with caffeine in it after midday.
• Declutter your bedroom to create a calm environment for sleep.
• Try to get up at the same time every day.
• Soak up some sun in the morning to give you a good hit of vitamin D.

chapters.title: Immunity isn’t just about your physical health, but your mental health too
chapters.text:
One of the most surprising aspects of the immune system is how it affects your mood. Not only does it protect you from disease and infection, but it also has a very strong link with your thoughts and feelings.

For instance, when you feel stressed out, it affects your mental health and makes you feel overwhelmed and down, creating a knock-on effect on your immunity, making you more susceptible to illness. When you’re not well, you also develop sickness behaviors designed to encourage you to put everything else to one side and take the time to rest and recuperate.

A study done on sepsis patients showed the link between the brain and the immune system. Those who survived a bout of sepsis often later presented to their doctor with cognitive problems, anxiety, and depression. This was put down to the significant immune response they had when they were fighting the sepsis infection. In addition, inflammation has been shown to be present in those who are depressed and appears to reduce when the depression abates.

Numerous scientific studies confirm the old adage that happiness and healthiness go hand in hand. ~ Dr. Jenna Macciochi

It’s equally as important to surround yourself with a loving and supportive network of people. Studies into loneliness have regularly shown that immunity is affected. That’s because when someone is lonely, their body goes into the famous ‘fight or flight’ mode, assuming there is a grave threat at hand. That causes an inflammatory response. However, there is no physical danger, so the inflammation sits there, causing problems because it’s not actually needed.

Stress is a massive problem in today’s society, and it’s something we need to focus on reducing. Stress causes inflammation, worsening of allergies, and mental health issues.

Reduce stress by exercising regularly, talking about problems, sleeping well, and socializing with those you care about.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The human body is a complex and sophisticated beast. The brain alone is still mostly unexplored by scientists and is capable of far more than we know right now. However, the immune system is also an extremely underrated and complicated system!

Without a strong and healthy immune system, you are open to infection, illness, disease, and all manner of other rather nasty events. It spans the entirety of your body; there isn’t a system or part of your body that it doesn’t touch. Even your emotional wellbeing is affected by your immune system.

Throughout this summary, you have learned some essential tricks to keep your immune system ticking along nicely. The biggest problem is that today’s modern society is so focused on rushing from one job to the next and never pausing that it negatively affects our health. So, doesn’t it make sense to try and strengthen it as much as possible?

Hopefully, the COVID-19 pandemic will teach people to slow down and take stock a little more often. We develop unhealthy habits that we don’t realize at the time, and these do nothing for our immunity. It’s crazy to realize that we’re basically pointing ourselves in the firing range towards illness by not looking after our immunity!

To protect your immune system, you simply need to focus on health and wellbeing. When you do that, you’ll feel a million times better and notice that you fall ill much less often too.

Try this

• Declutter your kitchen cupboards and restock them with healthy, organic produce. Perhaps even cook your own meals instead of ordering takeout!

• Make sleep an absolute priority, and don’t assume that tiredness is in fashion.

• Start a journal and write down anything that is bothering you on a daily basis.

Read it till the end?
